system. For example,  a man-in-the-middle attack [4] or a
jamming attack [5] could intercept and manipulate vehicle
communication and alter critical data,  leading to improper
actions  such as  sudden braking or  acceleration. This can
disrupt the platooning system’s reachable set and push it into
unsafe states; see Fig. 1 for illustrations. This issue is studied
through control  theory as a reachability problem, assessing

whether the system’s reachable set intersects predefined danger
zones [6], [7].
While attacks can severely impact a platoon, defense strate-
gies can mitigate these risks. For instance, the authors in [7],
[8] propose redesigning actuator bounds  to limit attackers’
capacity through actuator saturation, as physical constraints
prevent  actuators  from injecting excessive energy into the
system. Although this method enhances safety, it reduces the
system’s defense efficiency by restricting all actuators, limiting
its ability to maintain safety with minimal resource use.
Instead of  restricting all  actuators’ bounds,  focusing on
selecting a subset of critical actuators with the most significant
impact on the system’s controllability as defensive positions is
more effective. Limiting only these actuators’ bounds allows
the system’s safety and defense efficiency to be maintained and
balanced, even in the worst-case scenario where all actuators
are compromised.  If only some actuators are attacked,  this
defense strategy offers even greater efficiency, as only the key
actuators need to be managed. Therefore,  the primary focus
of this paper is determining the optimal number, placement,
and input bounds for these critical actuators to ensure vehicle
platooning safety.
A. Related Works and Research Problem
Various studies have focused on actuator saturation in the
control  theory field to prevent attacks or disturbances  that
could endanger  systems.  Studies  [7]  and [8] design new
physical limits on actuators to deter attackers from arbitrarily
manipulating the system,  and the authors  in [9],  [10] go
a step further and design limits on actuators such that  the
reachable set avoids dangerous states and includes desired
operation states. The authors  in [11]  study the possibility
of constraining the controller output  (i.e.,  the input  to the
actuators) utilizing a dynamic filter designed to prevent  the
reachability of dangerous plant  states. Recently,  they have
considered stealthy actuator and sensor attacks and proposed a
set-theoretic method to synthesize safety-preserving filters for
closed-loop systems [12].
While control theory offers insights into actuator saturation,
its application in vehicle platooning still needs to be explored.
In real-world contexts, actuators must balance both safety and

Moreover,  ui(k)  is  the  secondary control  strategy  that
relies on a communication network susceptible to jamming
attack [5] or other types of attacks to transmit system state
information and control  instructions. These attacks can lead
to unsafe scenarios,  such as reducing the distance between
adjacent vehicles below the safety threshold or causing a crash.
Because of the physical limits in the acceleration/deceleration
of each vehicle, we assume that the secondary control action
is bounded by ui ≤ ui(k) ≤ u¯i  [18]. By changing the
upper bound u¯i and lower bound ui of ui(k) to constrain
the acceleration of the vehicle, it is an essential method for us
to resist attacks.

metadata.section_title: Enhance Safety of Vehicle Platooning by a two-stage ASAP algorithm

text:
In this section, we propose a two-stage algorithm to solve
the ASAP problem (8).  Initially, we  involve  the optimal
actuator  locations in determining the approximate shape of
the reachable set for the defense strategy. Then, by solving
an optimization problem, we determine the input bounds and
scale  the sizes of  the  reachable set  to avoid  intersecting
with dangerous states. Thereby, we can ensure the safety of
the vehicle platooning system. Finally, we present  the final
algorithm to solve the ASAP problem.

TO APPEAR IN THE 2025 AMERICAN CONTROL CONFERENCE, DENVER, COLO
Figure 2.  Different ellipsoids encapsulating reachable set  (i.e., estimated
reachable sets) when selecting different actuator locations and input bounds
(only the first two dimensions are shown after projecting a 5-D ellipsoids into
a 2-D plane). The detailed simulation results are in Section IV-B.
A.  Impacts of Placement and Saturation on Reachable Set
We note that different combinations of actuator locations
and bounds yield different shapes and sizes of estimated reach-
able sets; see illustrated examples in Fig. 2. We can infer from
Fig. 2 that (i) one can effectively control the shape of reachable
set RS by strategically choosing the actuator location and (ii)
adjust the volume of RS by setting actuator bounds, ensuring
constraint  (8c) holds. Hence, we first consider  the actuator
placement problem in Section III-B, then followed by solving
the actuator saturation problem in Section III-C with the help
of system transformation by the decoupling of locations and
bounds in Section II-C.
B. Actuator Placement to Determine the Defense Locations
The actuator placement problem [15] aims to actuate a small
fraction of states of (6) to optimize system performances. In
the vehicle platooning system,  it selects the optimal defense
locations to maximize the vehicle platooning system’s con-
trollability to mitigate attacks. That is, if we do not consider
the actuation bounds Γˆ or equivalently consider  the actua-
tion bounds as unit-energy inputs, and the objective function
g(S, Γˆ) turns into g(S; Γˆ). Therefore, the actuator placement
problem can be written as
AP: min g(S; Γˆ) s. t. S ⊆ V, |S| ≤ m,  (9)

We note that a set  function g(·) is supermodular  if and
′  ′
only if g(S ∪ {e}) − g(S) ≤ g(S ∪ {e}) − g(S ) for any
′  ′
subsets S ⊆ S  ⊆ V and {e} ∈ V\S  [15],  [20]. A
set  function g(·)  is submodular  if −g(·)  is supermodular.
Intuitively,  submodularity is a diminishing returns property
where adding an element e to a smaller set gives a larger
gain than adding one to a larger set [20], [21].
The supermodularity framework in set function optimization
allows greedy algorithms, which are computationally efficient
and perform well  despite being suboptimal. Notably,  the
set  function g(S)  is supermodular  [15], making it  suitable
for a classic greedy algorithm that can solve the NP-hard
problem AP and yield a solution S with at least 63% of the
∗
optimal value g(S ) [22]. Empirical studies [20],  [22]–[24]
suggest that greedy algorithms can often achieve near-optimal
solutions, not just 63% of the optimal.
C. Actuator Saturation to Redesign Bounds
∗
With the optimal defense location S  for the AP problem
fixed  in BS,  the  reachable set RS defines  the distances
and velocities the vehicle platooning system can reach. This
section addresses the (AS) problem [7]–[9], aiming to prevent
collisions or excessive speeds—represented by the dangerous
set D —by redesigning the input bounds Γˆ.

Specifically, we
frame the AS problem as an optimization task to minimize
the trace of the new bounds Γˆ while ensuring the redesigned
ellipsoid for the reachable set does not intersect with D.
Note  that  although the  true reachable set RS of  (6)  is
cumbersome to formulate, we can find a minimum-volume
ellipsoid E(P , α) with P and α as the shape matrix and center
encapsulating reachable set. The ellipsoid E(P , α) is a kind of
over-approximations of the reachable set (i.e., RS). As long
as E(P , α) ∩ D = ∅, the constraint (8d) can be satisfied.

Next, we present a simple three-vehicle platooning system
to help readers intuitively understand our proposed method.
Then, we present a relatively large-scale system to present
our results further.
B. Three-vehicle Platooning System

This paper addresses maintaining safety and defense effi-
ciency in vehicle platooning systems under potential cyber-
attacks. By focusing on control node placement and optimiz-
ing input bounds for key actuators, we develop a defense
strategy that limits the impact of attacks while preserving the
system’s ability to operate effectively. Rather than restricting
all actuators with low defense efficiency, we demonstrate that
selectively constraining only critical actuators enhances safety.
Our analysis of the system’s reachable set under state and input
bounds shows that this approach provides a practical balance
between safety and defense efficiency in vehicle platooning.
Future work could explore the real-world implementation of
